draft_1_2.html:
NexPhase Advisory  ·  Power Market Intelligence

The Interconnect™

Weekly intelligence on power procurement, interconnection queues, and data center development — for teams building at the grid edge.

Week of June 14, 2026  ·  Vol. 1, No. 2 nexphaseadvisory.net

Data center development activity this week spans at least nine states and four grid regions, underscoring that siting pressure has moved well past the Northern Virginia core—with Tract and an undisclosed developer each proposing large campuses in Goochland County alone, while Fundamental Data advances a Mason County, West Virginia campus and Moonshot Infrastructure files site plans in Muncy, Pennsylvania. Capital markets remain open for well-structured deals, evidenced by Corscale and Affinius securing $975M from Blue Owl against a fully leased Northern Virginia asset, and Cipher Mining pursuing $810M in senior secured notes for its Stingray site in West Texas on the back of Amazon and Fluidstack pre-leases. Community resistance, however, is emerging as a genuine project risk: DC BLOX's Nashville proposal faces a 386,000-signature petition and a potential moratorium, Wonder Valley is pushing forward in Beaver County despite a local commissioner moratorium, and Panattoni is now seeking to recover cash from Turtle, Wisconsin on a stalled 430-acre project. For developers and operators, the week's read is straightforward—financing and hyperscaler demand are not the binding constraints, but local permitting and community opposition are increasingly capable of stalling or killing projects that looked viable on paper.

Power market intelligence from a practitioner with two decades in origination and interconnection — 5+ GW delivered to NTP across CAISO, PJM, MISO, ERCOT, and WECC. The analysis connects public news to what's actually moving in the queue.

Feature

Connector Markets — Load & Supply in the Same Geography

A "connector market" exists where a known data center load announcement overlaps with viable generation in the same ISO/state footprint. These are the highest-value origination windows — one conversation can structure both sides of a deal.

Interconnection

Data Center Site Selection Bypasses Queues as Capacity Crunch Deepens

A wave of developer moves this week reveals that the interconnection queue is now a secondary constraint—capital structure, behind-the-meter generation, and local permitting have become the primary battlegrounds for large-load siting.

The most consequential pattern visible in this week's signals is not any single project announcement but the collective strategy emerging across developers to route around the interconnection queue entirely. The clearest example is the Stark Power acquisition of Sagebrush sites, where Stark raised up to $140M to fund the acquisition, with terms undisclosed, specifically to control co-located natural-gas-fired power plants capable of serving load through behind-the-meter gas generation bypassing interconnection queues. That approach—own the generation, skip the queue—is becoming a template rather than an exception, and it has direct implications for how IPPs and power developers should think about asset value in the current cycle.

On the hyperscale lease side, the financing activity this week underscores how rapidly capital is moving to lock in capacity ahead of queue resolution. Corscale and Affinius secured $975M in financing from Blue Owl for a fully leased Northern Virginia data center, a transaction that reflects lender comfort with contracted load even in a region where PJM interconnection timelines remain extended. Campus Announcements — Scale & Power Implications

Hut 8 Locks 352MW Nueces County Hyperscaler Lease
Hut 8's Beacon Point campus in Nueces County, Texas secures a 352MW hyperscaler lease backed by a $4.25B note offering.

Hut 8's Nueces County campus, internally branded Beacon Point, is emerging as one of the largest single-site load commitments in ERCOT this cycle. The 352MW hyperscaler lease signals a fully contracted anchor tenant at a scale that will demand serious transmission study work from ERCOT staff, given the coastal Texas grid's existing congestion profile and the sheer size of the incremental load block.

The $4.25B note offering tied to the same campus underscores how capital markets are now willing to underwrite large-scale AI infrastructure at the project level rather than the corporate balance sheet. For interconnection practitioners, the combination of a named hyperscaler offtake and a discrete debt instrument sets a structuring precedent that other merchant campus developers in ERCOT will likely attempt to replicate.

Goochland County, Virginia Draws Two Separate Campus Proposals
Tract and the undisclosed Tuckahoe Tech Park developer both propose large data center campuses in Goochland County, Virginia near the West Creek corridor.

PJM's Northern Virginia constrained zone is already well-documented, but the West Creek corridor in Goochland County is attracting a second wave of large proposals as developers seek land with transmission adjacency outside the most congested Loudoun County pockets. Tract's proposal and the separately filed 900-acre Tuckahoe Tech Park—whose developer remains undisclosed—represent meaningful incremental queue pressure on Dominion Energy Virginia's transmission system in the same geographic pocket.

Two large proposals in the same county filing window will force local planners and Dominion interconnection staff to think carefully about cumulative substation loading and whether existing 500kV and 230kV infrastructure can absorb both projects on overlapping timelines. Goochland's relative greenfield status compared to Ashburn makes it attractive, but the Tuckahoe Tech Park's 900-acre footprint alone suggests a multi-gigawatt build-out ambition that will require its own network upgrade study.

Applied Digital Signs Fifth Campus With Repeat Hyperscaler at 210MW
Applied Digital's new 210MW North Dakota campus is leased to the same hyperscaler already occupying three of its existing AI campuses.

Applied Digital's announcement that a single hyperscaler tenant has now absorbed capacity across four of its campuses—including the newly signed 210MW North Dakota site—points to a deepening bilateral relationship that goes well beyond a spot lease. For load-serving entities and regional transmission organizations watching North Dakota load growth, the cumulative MW commitment from this one tenant relationship deserves close attention as a driver of regional generation and transmission need.

The repeat-tenant structure also carries project finance implications: a demonstrated pattern of lease renewals and expansions from the same creditworthy offtaker substantially de-risks the debt stack on new campuses before a single rack is energized. Developers competing in the hyperscaler supply chain will note that Applied Digital's model—purpose-built AI campuses anchored by a single large tenant—is now five campuses deep and still expanding.

Cipher Mining Seeks $810M for West Texas Stingray Site
Cipher Mining is pursuing $810M in senior secured notes to finance its Stingray data center site in West Texas, which already holds Amazon and Fluidstack pre-leases.

PPA & Financing

Market Pricing Context

The high-yield debt market is clearly open for West Texas AI infrastructure plays right now, and the pricing signals are instructive. Cipher Mining is seeking $810M in senior secured notes to finance its Stingray site in West Texas, backstopped by Amazon and Fluidstack pre-leases — a structure that mirrors how developers are using anchor hyperscaler offtake to unlock investment-grade-adjacent debt terms on what would otherwise be speculative merchant builds. Hut 8's Nueces County campus (the 352MW hyperscaler lease tied to its Beacon Point campus) is pursuing a $4.25B note offering on the same basic logic: contracted load de-risks the capital structure enough to support nine-figure-plus debt tranches at acceptable spreads. On the equity and mezz side, Corscale and Affinius just closed a $975M Blue Owl financing for a fully leased Northern Virginia asset, confirming that 'fully leased at signing' remains the threshold at which institutional private credit will write very large checks without requiring construction completion risk to clear first. The common thread across all three: hyperscaler or named-tenant lease coverage is now the underwriting prerequisite, not a nice-to-have.

PPA economics in ERCOT and PJM are being shaped by the sheer volume of load announcements hitting both grids simultaneously — Nueces County, the Tract and Tuckahoe Tech Park proposals clustering around Goochland/West Creek in PJM, Fundamental Data in Mason County WV, and Moonshot in Muncy PA all competing for the same constrained interconnection queue positions and the same pool of uncontracted renewable capacity. For PPA analysts, this matters because it sets an implicit price ceiling on utility-scale renewable PPAs in constrained markets — if a developer can source behind-the-meter gas at an all-in cost below the queue-delivered renewable alternative, that becomes the competitive benchmark. Applied Digital's model, now with a 210MW hyperscaler lease on its fifth AI campus and three campuses leased to the same tenant, also signals that repeat-tenant concentration is becoming a financing feature rather than a credit risk, as lenders treat the repeat relationship as implicit offtake optionality on future phases.

warns.issue: The lede states DC BLOX's proposal is in 'Nashville' but the source data identifies the project as DC BLOX's Nashville data center proposal. However, a separate DC Blox entry places a project in 'Irvington, Indiana'. The Nashville attribution for DC BLOX appears consistent with the source, so no issue there. However, the lede attributes the 386,000-signature petition to DC BLOX's 'Nashville proposal' — the source data confirms this (386K+ signatures, Nashville). No flag needed here.

The lede says 'at least nine states' but the source data covers Virginia (×2), Texas (×3), New York, Utah, Indiana, Tennessee, West Virginia, Pennsylvania, Wisconsin, North Dakota — that is at least 10 distinct states, making 'nine states' a potentially understated figure that contradicts the source data.
The lede describes DC BLOX's Nashville project as a '386,000-signature petition.' The source states '386K+ signatures,' which rounds to at least 386,000, so this is consistent. No flag.

warns.section: Applied Digital Signs Fifth Campus With Repeat Hyperscaler at 210MW
warns.issue: The generated text states the hyperscaler has 'absorbed capacity across four of its campuses' and that the model is 'five campuses deep', but the source data says Applied Digital is 'developing fifth AI campus with three leased to same tenant' — implying only three existing campuses are leased to the same hyperscaler, not four. The generated text overstates the number of campuses leased to the repeat hyperscaler.

Source data identifies DC BLOX's project location as Nashville, Tennessee, but the source's DC Blox entry (separate record) places their project in Irvington, Indiana. The Nashville entry is correctly attributed to 'DC BLOX' so the entity is not fabricated, but the note conflates DC BLOX Nashville with the broader DC Blox Indiana project — no contradiction severe enough to block, but the framing is accurate per the Nashville-specific source record.

The note describes the corridor as 'Northern Virginia exurban' but Goochland County is in the Richmond metro area, not Northern Virginia. This is a geographic mischaracterization that contradicts reasonable practitioner knowledge and the source data's plain location reference.

The note states Cipher's '$810M debt raise tied to pre-leases from Amazon and Fluidstack.' The source data describes it as '$810M senior secured notes to finance Stingray data center site after Amazon and Fluidstack pre-leases' — the causal framing ('tied to pre-leases') is a minor analytical inference beyond what the source states, but not a hard fabrication.

The note references 'Beacon Point AI's Nueces County plans' in the context of 'ERCOT West and South zones.' Beacon Point AI is a known verified fact (Nueces County, Texas), so the name is not flagged, but grouping both projects as creating pressure on 'ERCOT West and South zones' together is an analytical conclusion not directly supported by source data and could mislead on zone-specific grid impact.

Stark Power's Sagebrush acquisition is described as being in ERCOT/PJM constrained queue context, but the source data does not specify which grid/region the Sagebrush assets are located in. This is not itself a flag. However, the generated text states Stark Power raised 'up to $140M raised with terms undisclosed' — this matches the KNOWN VERIFIED FACTS whitelist and should not be flagged.

The generated text states Applied Digital's 'three campuses leased to the same tenant' — the source data says 'three leased to same tenant' which matches. No flag needed here. However, the text also characterizes 'repeat-tenant concentration is becoming a financing feature rather than a credit risk' and 'lenders treat the repeat relationship as implicit offtake optionality on future phases' — these are analytical conclusions not contradicted by source data and are practitioner-level framing. Not flagged.

The generated text describes the Corscale/$975M Blue Owl deal as being for 'a fully leased Northern Virginia asset' and states it confirms 'fully leased at signing' is the institutional private credit threshold. The source confirms the deal and the fully leased status. However, the interpretive claim that this confirms a universal threshold for institutional private credit writing 'very large checks without requiring construction completion risk to clear first' is an analytical conclusion beyond what the source states — a practitioner addition that could be misleading if taken as fact.

"Applied Digital" appears in 4 sections
"Corscale" appears in 4 sections
"DC BLOX" appears in 3 sections
"Wonder Valley" appears in 3 sections
"Goochland County" appears in 3 sections

Return ONLY a JSON array, no markdown.' + '

SUPPLY AGGREGATES (per ISO, live queue): ' + JSON.stringify($('Aggregate Queue MW').first().json.byRegion) + '

SIGNALS:
' + JSON.stringify($('Select Signals').first().json.top14) }] }) }}
